questions.text: What are Automations in Kiro Web?

options.label: Automated recurring tasks that run in a cloud sandbox, cloning repos and opening PRs with changes
Local scripts that execute on the developer's terminal at scheduled times
CI/CD workflows configured directly in GitHub Actions
Keyboard macros that automate command sequences in the editor

questions.explanation: Automations in Kiro Web are automated recurring tasks that run in a cloud sandbox. They clone repositories, execute user-defined tasks, and automatically open Pull Requests with the changes made.

questions.text: Automations execute tasks in the developer's local machine sandbox.

options.label: True
False

questions.explanation: Automations run in a cloud sandbox, completely isolated from the developer's local environment. This ensures security, reproducibility, and allows execution even when the developer's machine is not running.

questions.text: What is the final result of an Automation execution?

options.label: A PDF report sent via email to the team
A Pull Request is automatically opened with the changes made
A direct commit to the main branch without review
A Slack notification with a summary of suggested changes

questions.explanation: The final result of an Automation execution is a Pull Request automatically opened with the changes made. This maintains the code review flow and allows the team to review changes before merging.

questions.text: Your documentation becomes outdated every week after merges to main. How to use Automations to solve this?

options.label: Configure a postToolUse hook that updates docs after each edit
Create an Automation with a weekly schedule that checks gaps between code and documentation, opening a PR with updates
Add a rule in the steering file to always update docs
Create a GitHub Action that blocks merges without updated docs

questions.explanation: The ideal solution is to create an Automation with a daily or weekly schedule that checks gaps between changed code in main and existing documentation, automatically opening a PR when something needs updating.

questions.text: How many schedules can be configured per Automation and which formats are accepted?

options.label: Only 1 schedule per automation, only in cron format
Up to 3 schedules per automation, only with built-in options (daily, weekly, monthly)
Up to 5 schedules per automation, accepting built-in options (daily, weekly) or custom cron expressions
Unlimited schedules per automation, but only in ISO 8601 format

questions.explanation: Each Automation supports up to 5 schedules, offering flexibility in configuration. Accepted formats include simplified built-in options (like daily and weekly) and custom cron expressions for greater control over execution times.

questions.text: You want to detect files changed in main without test coverage and automatically generate tests every Tuesday. How to configure?

options.label: Create a hook that runs a coverage report after each push
Configure a GitHub Action with a cron schedule for Tuesdays
Create an Automation selecting the repo, describing the task to detect files without coverage, and configuring a cron schedule for every Tuesday
Add a skill that analyzes coverage when manually invoked

questions.explanation: The ideal configuration uses Automations: select the repository, describe the task (detect changed files without test coverage and generate necessary tests), and configure the schedule with a cron expression for execution every Tuesday.
